title: How does LAIN not get the same amount of stick as EVA
display_title: How does LAIN not get the same amount of stick as EVA
wikitext:
[]: How does LAIN not get the same amount of stick as EVA?             Quote Post by psychedelic kid » Fri Jul 02, 2010 9:29 am I have often found criticism of evangelion to be superficial, baked with frustration and even contradictory. It's considered pretentious, just an incoherent ramble, and more formidably "not deep, just ambiguous so it gives that illusion." I'll agree with first part however i often believe if it isn't a little pretentious I"m just watching DBZ, but the others defiantly no. But how does LAIN not get the same amount of crap, especially when you can apply the later two to LAIN even more? It is easy deal with someone who dislike EVA because they prefer Naruto or something like that, but I hear people call EVA pretentious and incoherent but also have LAIN in their favorites, How does that work? The message behind LAIN is not all that deep, it is something fairly reactionary to be perfectly honest, yet you are forced to over-analyze it like hell. Thing are purposely muddled up chronologically and physically, its some may find it hard to decipher. Plus the message doesn't seem to really have the writer's soul, you can tell that Anno deeply cared about what he was writing about, you can tell it was his artistic expression, the writer of LAIN admitted he wanted it for intellectual discussion. I have a few theories why some say they hate EVA but like LAIN. Firstly they don't even properly like LAIN, for your cliche anime fan it is unrelatable, so they view it has some peculiar alien planet so they might as well say the like it. While EVA while they may not connect with the whole psychology and philosophy stuff, it has all those fight scenes and characters such as Asuka, so they can connect with it at a limited level making them merely frustrated. Secondly, it obviously seems to makeup for not liking EVA. They want to distance themselves from the hentai jerking naruto fan so they go for the 'other smart anime' to have a leg to stand on. Thirdly, although both EVA and LAIN and pretty grim(LAIN perhaps being even grimmer), but EVA tell something dark about themselves. Reminds me of people preferring Jung over Freud(comparison is appropriate due to both shows use of psychology lol) although i also prefer Jung, it seems queer how some prefer Jung to Freud, despite jung having less evidence and realism than Freud(which is their reason for not liking him) by going in to the metaphysical, because Freud tells something upsetting and disturbing about them. Fourth although there is a lot of WAPENESE IDIOTS or Japanese who are bigoted about their culture, who are also EVA fans, but those who are not will obviously like LAIN hearing on what the writer said about the whole culture wars, with the whole "ooo manga is better than western comics" vibe within anime fans. I want to clarify I am not dissing(love a bit of slang) LAIN, and there are many people who like EVA and LAIN, and LAIN also gets the brunk of the pretentious insult. But not as much as EVA, and it seems fans who like LAIN but have a vendetta against EVA are massive hypocrites(has anyone been reminded of hippos when they hear that word?) P.S I'm not sure if this topic is in the right place or not so sorry in advance

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Fri Jul 02, 2010 9:46 am ITT: silly assumptions, nasty provocations and the weird implication of Lain not being criticized.

[]: [insert hilarious pun here]             Quote Post by Killer Bee » Fri Jul 02, 2010 10:08 am Lain doesn't have semen. /topic

[]:             Quote Post by psychedelic kid » Fri Jul 02, 2010 10:47 am lol my theories were ironic being a parody of over assumptions and weird theories of EVA, but how were they nasty? and LAIN does get less criticism than EVA

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Fri Jul 02, 2010 11:09 am psychedelic kid wrote: but how were they nasty?

[]:             Quote Post by psychedelic kid » Fri Jul 02, 2010 11:24 am the only way I can see myself as nasty is when i was using the stereotypes, this is different from me generalizing(e.g all anine fans are...), or being personal, only then i can be seen as nasty

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Fri Jul 02, 2010 11:40 am Evangelion begins as a giant robot show with great action sequences and above average (/downright brilliant as the case may be) directing, while content-wise it gives the impression that it plays close to its shonen genre and its audience’s expectations. Slapstick comedy, fanservice, and a reasonable emphasis on giant figures beating the tar out of each other seemingly dominate the first half of the series, before the second half comes along and gets grimdark srs bidniss and subverts, inverts, or just immolates almost everything about the first half. In short, Eva fools viewers into thinking it’s no big deal, and then turns into colossal mindfuck full of artfag editing skilz, subversive characters, and strange plot twists (or holes) that have come to be recognized as extremely influential and have subsequently popped up in a LOT of series. Plus a bit of psychoanalysis and philosophy added into the mix for lulz. Lain, on the other hand, kicks off all grimdark and srs from the get-go. Lain pretty much sets itself up as being off-beat artfag-like shit, has been marketed as such, and validated itself as being such throughout its whole run. In other words, Lain is intentionally off-putting from the very beginning, similar to another ABe/Ueda (and crew) work, Texhnolyze, but not quite to the same extreme. It isn’t a case of playing close to shonen tropes before deconstructing or subverting them; it’s a case of presenting itself as weird, arty, and abstract in the first episode, and then being consistently weird, arty, and abstract for its whole duration. Lain still gets shit slung in its face for being pretentious and arty, but not as much as NGE does simply because NGE is a lot more popular and more accessible.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Fri Jul 02, 2010 12:14 pm Merridian wrote: Evangelion begins as a giant robot show with great action sequences and above average (/downright brilliant as the case may be) directing, while content-wise it gives the impression that it plays close to its shonen genre and its audience’s expectations. Slapstick comedy, fanservice, and a reasonable emphasis on giant figures beating the tar out of each other seemingly dominate the first half of the series, before the second half comes along and gets grimdark srs bidniss and subverts, inverts, or just immolates almost everything about the first half.

[]:             Quote Post by Dark doom » Fri Jul 02, 2010 12:20 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: This. The beginning and ending of Evangelion are TOTALLY different. Lain is crazy and trippy throughout. It's very honest and structured that way. It does progress, but it doesn't end on a totally different tone.

[]:             Quote Post by psychedelic kid » Fri Jul 02, 2010 2:05 pm read my post, my beef is those who like LAIN but hate EVA for reasons that could be completely related to LAIN as well perhaps im chasing a red herring here....

[]:             Quote Post by The Triforce Fighter » Sat Jul 03, 2010 7:14 am I do think it just comes down to the fact that Evangelion starts of as your typical mech anime and then does a 360 half way through. I mean if you start watching Evangelion because of the mech fights you'll be disappointed too after it becomes more about the minds of the characters apposed to UNIT-01 beating Angels up (unless like myself you like phycology and giant mechs) However as Lain from the start warns you its going to be a rather crazy experience it gives the viewer a bit of pre warning for what to expect. Unlike Evangelion which to some may have found the phycological expects like being suddenly having a bucket of cold water thrown over their head. I do think what Psychedelic Kid said about Eva's dark nature may have also been a turn off for some people as although the plot maybe be rather bizarre the characters are very human and break traditional anime stereotypes.

[]:             Quote Post by berto » Sat Jul 03, 2010 2:45 pm Well, think about the nature of Evangelion. While the is a critique on then modern Japan, where ther characters who were flawed and suffering from a major psycological problem and the only two 'ideal' characters were killed, Evangelion's nature tends to be ambiguous, just look at the tv ending, hell, just look at the end of the movie. When it comes to the characters the show endend with a clear sence of acomplishment, however the science fiction and action parts of the show never really concluded. People's biggest beef with Evangelion is it's ending. Lain has a very clear, and you could say happy, ending. Any of those people complaining about Evangelion enjoyed the show all the way to episode 24 and were hoping that there was something in the last two episodes that would explain all of the things that the show build up, but they didn't. It's that simple.

[]: Re: How does LAIN not get the same amount of stick as EVA?             Quote Post by Enki v.2 » Mon Jul 05, 2010 5:07 pm psychedelic kid wrote: The message behind LAIN is not all that deep

[]: Re: How does LAIN not get the same amount of stick as EVA?             Quote Post by Oz » Tue Jul 06, 2010 2:17 am Enki v.2 wrote: Which one? I come up with a totally new batch every time I watch it.

[]:             Quote Post by Dr. Nick » Wed Jul 07, 2010 1:26 pm Thirding what Merridian said. It's mostly about audience expectations. I think a bigger question might be, why aren't Alien Nine and Narutaru more reviled? They start out as Pokemon but end with a good dose of "what the fuck am I watching?".

[]:             Quote Post by child of Lilith » Wed Jul 07, 2010 4:58 pm Neither of those titles is well known enough to garner the type of hate Eva can generate. Enough people have to know about something before a general consensus for or against it can be made.
